topic: Two strangers realize they've been dating the same person
raw_output:
[story 1]

The shared apartment smelled faintly of burnt toast and something vaguely floral, a combination that made my stomach clench. I was standing by the kitchen counter, trying to decide whether to leave immediately, when he walked in. His silhouette was unmistakable, the way he tilted his head when he was concentrating on something—a tiny, almost imperceptible gesture that screamed familiarity. My jaw tightened, a hard knot forming beneath my skin. When he turned, his eyes met mine, and for a split second, a strange, awful recognition flickered between us. I immediately looked away, focusing intently on a smudge on the granite countertop, my knuckles whitening as I gripped the edge. The air felt suddenly thick, pressurized.

[story 2]

I watched from across the crowded gallery opening, a glass of lukewarm champagne trembling slightly in my hand. Then I saw him. He was leaning against a pillar, laughing at something a woman said, his posture exactly as I had observed him last Tuesday, when we’d shared a pretentious wine tasting. A wave of profound unease washed over me, making my breath catch in my throat. I forced myself to move, taking a deliberate step back, the expensive fabric of my dress suddenly feeling like a straitjacket. When he finally glanced over, his smile was bright, open, and utterly devoid of the guardedness I had cultivated. The sheer, blatant familiarity of his expression made me want to physically recoil.

[story 3]

It happened over lukewarm coffee in the breakroom. I was staring at the chipped paint on the water cooler when I noticed him approaching. The sound of his footsteps, that specific, slightly clumsy rhythm, sent a jolt of unpleasantness through me. He stopped beside me, placing a hand on my shoulder in that familiar, proprietary way. My muscles instantly tensed, pulling inward as if bracing for impact. I didn't meet his gaze; instead, I focused on the pattern of the linoleum floor, tracing the grout lines with my eyes. When he murmured something low and casual, something only we would ever share, a hot flush rose on my neck, and I felt an intense need to vacate the space, to dissolve into the background noise of the room.
